name: Anubis (embalming)
shortDescription: Anubis was the Egyptian god of embalming and the afterlife. He guided souls into the underworld and oversaw the weighing of the heart ceremony.
description: Anubis, often depicted with the head of a jackal and the body of a man, was a central figure in ancient Egyptian funerary rites and beliefs about the afterlife. His primary role was as the god of embalming, a practice crucial to preserving the body for its journey into the underworld. He oversaw the mummification process, ensuring the proper preservation of the deceased. Anubis was also responsible for guiding souls through the treacherous pathways of the underworld and presenting them to Osiris for judgment. During the Weighing of the Heart ceremony, Anubis would oversee the weighing of the deceased's heart against the feather of Ma'at (truth and justice). If the heart was lighter than the feather, the soul was deemed worthy to enter the afterlife. If heavier, it would be devoured by Ammit. Anubis's association with jackals likely stemmed from the animals' scavenging habits around cemeteries, making them closely linked to death and the dead in the minds of the ancient Egyptians. He was a guardian of tombs and protector of the deceased, ensuring their safe passage into the realm of the gods. His prominence in funerary rituals reflects the Egyptians' deep concern with death and the afterlife, and their belief in the importance of preserving the body for eternity.
appearance: Jackal-headed man, often depicted with black fur, representing the decay of the body and the fertile soil of the Nile valley.
abilities: Overseeing the Weighing of the Heart ceremony

sources.title: Ancient Egyptian Literature: A Book of Readings
sources.author: Miriam Lichtheim

sources.title: Myths and Symbols of Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Lucia Gahlin
